What We Monitor / Agitators & Mixers

Asset type

Agitator & mixer monitoring

Agitators run inside sealed reactors, pressurised vessels, and hygienic mixing tanks. Variable loads, inaccessible bearings, and hazardous environments make them one of the hardest asset types to monitor. SAM4 measures from the motor control cabinet, no vessel access, no process interruption.

Talk to an engineer See what we detect

Why monitor agitators and mixers

Inside a sealed vessel. Invisible to inspection. Critical to production.

Agitators sit inside sealed, pressurised, or hazardous vessels. Physical inspection requires isolation, draining, and confined space entry. Most agitators run without any condition monitoring until product quality drops or a seal leaks.

30–50%

of agitator maintenance events are seal failures: the single largest maintenance cost. Industry data shows improper installation and contamination drive most premature failures.

€5K–200K

per gearbox failure event. Agitator gearboxes handle axial thrust from the impeller, radial loads from shaft deflection, and process-induced torque spikes.

0%

of agitators in a typical chemical plant have continuous condition monitoring. Most rely on monthly walk-arounds or run-to-failure.

How ESA works on agitators

Motor current as a window into a sealed vessel

ESA measures the electrical current drawn by the agitator motor at the MCC. Every mechanical event in the drivetrain, from a worn gear tooth to an imbalanced impeller, modulates the motor's torque demand. That modulation appears as characteristic patterns in the current signal.

Motor-side faults: strong signal

Motor bearings, stator winding degradation, rotor bar defects, and eccentricity produce signatures at frequencies related to the motor's rotation speed (typically 1450–1500 RPM). These are fully visible regardless of the gearbox ratio or impeller speed.

Gearbox faults: moderate-strong signal

Gear mesh frequency and its sidebands appear in the motor current spectrum. Published research validates detection of gear tooth defects through motor current analysis, even under variable load conditions. Gearbox bearing faults closer to the motor side propagate more strongly.

Impeller-side faults: limited signal

Impeller speed (typically 10–300 RPM after the gearbox) determines the sideband offset from the supply frequency. Above ~60 RPM, blade-pass and bearing defect frequencies are resolvable. Below 60 RPM, 1× impeller-speed sidebands fall too close to the fundamental for reliable detection. Multi-stage gearboxes further attenuate these signals.

What SAM4 detects on agitators and mixers

Seven fault types. Honest confidence ratings.

Fault type What's happening How SAM4 detects it Typical lead time Confidence
Mechanical unbalance Deposit on paddles, erosion, or damaged impeller creating rotational mass asymmetry 1× rotational frequency sideband in motor current spectrum Months Full
Misalignment Motor-to-gearbox shaft or coupling misalignment 2× rotational frequency sideband in motor current Weeks Full
Motor bearing failure Motor-side bearing wear from unbalance, misalignment, or lubrication breakdown Eccentricity and bearing defect frequency sidebands in rotor current Weeks Conditional*
Motor stator/rotor faults Insulation breakdown, broken rotor bars from thermal cycling or high starting currents Current imbalance between phases, characteristic (1±2s)f sidebands Months Full
Gearbox gear faults Gear tooth wear, pitting, spalling from fatigue or underloading Gear mesh frequency sidebands in current spectrum. GMF = teeth × shaft RPM / 60 Weeks Full
Active power trends Process load change, viscosity shifts, settled solids, or developing mechanical issue Progressive deviation in mean power consumption at equivalent operating conditions Continuous Full
Coupling faults Coupling eccentricity or misalignment Combined eccentricity and rotational frequency patterns Weeks Full

* Conditional: Motor-side bearing detection is reliable on direct-coupled agitators. Sensitivity reduces significantly on drive trains with reduction gearbox and splitter configurations. On complex drive trains, combine ESA with vibration monitoring.

Detection capability depends on drive topology, operating speed, load variation, and gearbox configuration.

Detection boundaries

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. These are the known limitations for ESA-based monitoring on agitators and mixers.

Complex drive trains. Motor → gearbox → splitter box configurations attenuate driven-end fault signals. Each transmission stage filters torque modulation from the mixer bearings. This is the primary limitation. It accounts for three of four known misses in Samotics' agitator CI data.
Variable batch loading. Batch agitators see dramatically varying load profiles: empty vessel, filling, full mixing, product change. Each phase produces different power profiles. SAM4 segments operating states and maintains separate baselines. Detection confidence is higher on continuous applications (wastewater digesters) than on batch reactors.
Low impeller speed. Impeller-speed faults (1× rotation) below ~60 RPM produce sidebands too close to the supply frequency fundamental for reliable detection. Blade-pass and gear-mesh frequencies remain resolvable at lower speeds because they occur at multiples of shaft speed.
Seal failure. Not directly detectable via ESA. Seal wear produces no electrical signature. ESA detects the mechanical precursors that accelerate seal failure: shaft runout, misalignment, and imbalance. But not the seal itself.
Magnetic drive couplings. Magnetic couplings (common on pharmaceutical bottom-entry mixers) mechanically decouple the motor from the impeller. Motor-side faults remain visible. Impeller-side faults do not propagate back through the magnetic coupling.
Small motors (<5 kW). Signal-to-noise ratio on small agitator motors limits frequency-specific fault detection. Many side-entry and bottom-entry mixers fall in the 1–10 kW range. ESA's value proposition weakens for these small units.

Based on 20 resolved agitator and mixer condition incidents and published MCSA research. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

What ESA covers. What the alternatives cover. Where they overlap.

Agitators operate in environments where vibration sensors often can't reach: sealed reactors, ATEX zones, GMP clean rooms. ESA doesn't replace driven-end vibration monitoring on critical agitators. It provides continuous motor-side monitoring in environments where vibration isn't practical, and screens fleets where per-unit vibration is uneconomical.

ESA leads

  • Motor electrical faults (stator, rotor bars, eccentricity)
  • Active power trending and process load profiling
  • Gearbox gear faults via gear mesh frequency analysis
  • Energy consumption and efficiency monitoring
  • Fleet-wide screening from the MCC
  • Monitoring in ATEX zones, GMP clean rooms, sealed vessels

Both contribute

  • Motor-to-gearbox misalignment (ESA: torque modulation. Vibration: 2× radial)
  • Mechanical unbalance (ESA: 1× sideband. Vibration: 1× radial, where accessible)
  • Gearbox bearing faults on motor side (ESA: current sidebands. Vibration: direct acceleration)
  • Coupling looseness and wear

Alternative leads

  • Gearbox bearing faults on output side (vibration on gearbox housing)
  • Shaft whip and deflection (vibration at shaft bearing locations)
  • Seal condition (seal flush pressure, temperature, leak detection)
  • Impeller erosion (visual inspection, efficiency measurement)
  • Gearbox wear metals and oil degradation (oil analysis)

The access problem defines the monitoring architecture

Chemical reactors, pharmaceutical vessels, and digester tanks are sealed, often pressurised, sometimes at elevated temperature. Placing vibration sensors on mixer bearings inside these vessels is impractical or prohibited. ATEX-certified sensors are 2–5× the cost of standard units. GMP environments restrict cable management and surface-mount equipment.

ESA installs at the MCC in under 60 minutes. No vessel penetration. No confined space entry. No interference with cleaning or sterilisation. For fleet monitoring across 20–50 agitators, ESA provides continuous screening at a fraction of the per-unit cost of wired vibration.

On critical reactor agitators where unplanned failure halts a production line, the strongest stack combines ESA (motor health, gearbox screening, power trending) with vibration (driven-end bearings, shaft dynamics) and seal monitoring (direct seal condition). ESA and vibration overlap narrowly. The complementary coverage is wide.

Agitator and mixer detections

Real faults caught on agitators and mixers

From Samotics' condition incident database. 20 resolved incidents across chemicals, water, waste processing, and pulp & paper.

POWER TRENDING Water & Wastewater

Gradual power rise on wastewater stirrer

Progressive active power increase detected on a slow-speed wastewater stirrer at Waterschap Rivierenland. Power trending revealed a developing mechanical issue before operators noticed any change. Demonstrates ESA's ability to catch slow trends even on low-speed assets. When the fault produces a torque effect.

CI-685 detection detail →
ELECTRICAL Chemicals

Supply frequency anomaly on tank mixer

SAM4 flagged an increase around supply frequency components on a Vopak Europoort tank mixer. Supply frequency analysis revealed an electrical anomaly at the chemical storage terminal. Early detection prevented escalation to motor winding damage.

CI-651 detection detail →
RECURRING Waste Processing

Repeated detections on agitator drive

Two separate condition incidents detected on the same Eurac UK agitator drive. Recurring alerts confirmed ongoing monitoring value and enabled the maintenance team to schedule interventions during planned downtime windows.

CI-2879 / CI-1460 detection detail →
BEARING Water & Wastewater

Digester mixer fault detection

Fault detected on a Southern Water primary digester mixer running in continuous operation. Stable baseline from continuous duty enabled clear anomaly identification. Continuous agitators provide the most reliable ESA baselines.

CI-3949 detection detail →

Installation on agitators and mixers

Under 60 minutes. No access to the vessel required.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

SAM4 installs at the MCC, the same panel your electricians already access. No confined space entry, no scaffolding, no process shutdown.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto the existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to the asset's operation.

3

Connect and commission

The SAM4 gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately, with first diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →

Where agitators and mixers are monitored

Industries using SAM4 on agitators and mixers

Chemicals

Reactor agitators, blending vessels, mixing tanks. Monitoring inside sealed and hazardous process environments where ATEX compliance restricts sensor installation.

Water & Wastewater

Digester mixers, stirrers, and aeration basin equipment. Continuous operation provides stable baselines for reliable trend detection.

Pulp & Paper

Pulper agitators, dump chest agitators, and stock preparation mixers. High-torque applications with variable loading from changing stock consistency.

Food & Beverage

Blending, mixing, and agitation in hygienic production environments. GMP constraints make MCC-based monitoring the practical choice.

Related asset types

Other asset types SAM4 monitors

Pumps

Centrifugal, submersible, and process pumps

Compressors

Reciprocating, screw, and centrifugal compressors

Fans & Blowers

Axial, centrifugal, and process air fans

Transmissions

Gearboxes, couplings, and belt drives
